narration: Last stop: the new test file. Four tests, covering the create path, rotate isolating between users, rotate returning zero, and touch updating the timestamp. The good news: the cross-user isolation test — creating sessions for users seven and eight, rotating seven, and asserting eight survives — is exactly the test I'd want. That's the regression you'd hate to miss. The gap I see: there's no test for the interaction in roles.py. We test that SessionStore.rotate() works, but not that update_role() actually calls it. A bug where someone removes the rotate() call from roles.py would slip through this test suite. Also, the touch() test stops at calling touch — it doesn't assert that rotated_at actually changed. So we know it doesn't crash, but not that it does the thing.

concerns.text: No integration test for the update_role -> session_store.rotate wiring. Removing the rotate call from roles.py would not break any test.
concerns.suggested_question: Should there be a test in test_roles.py that update_role() invalidates the user's sessions? Right now the wire-up is uncovered.

concerns.text: test_touch_updates_rotated_at calls touch() but asserts nothing about the result.
concerns.suggested_question: test_touch_updates_rotated_at doesn't actually assert rotated_at changed — should it read back the row and compare timestamps?

look_closer_for: The tmp_db fixture — confirm it provides table isolation between tests so create() in one test doesn't leak into rotate() in another

segments.text: Last stop: the new test file. Four tests, covering the create path, rotate isolating between users, rotate returning zero, and touch updating the timestamp. The good news: the cross-user isolation test — creating sessions for users seven and eight, rotating seven, and asserting eight survives — is exactly the test I'd want.
That's the regression you'd hate to miss. The gap I see: there's no test for the interaction in roles.py. We test that SessionStore.rotate() works, but not that update_role() actually calls it.

A bug where someone removes the rotate() call from roles.py would slip through this test suite. Also, the touch() test stops at calling touch — it doesn't assert that rotated_at actually changed. So we know it doesn't crash, but not that it does the thing.
